title: From Lydenï؟½s ï؟½Ireniusï؟½ - Act III. Sc. II.
content:
آ آ آ آ Gow. Had it been your Prince instead of a groom caught in this noose thereï؟½s not an astrologer of the city, 
آ آ آ آ Prince. Sacked! Sacked! We were a city yesterday.
آ آ آ آ Gow. So be it, but I was not governor. Not an astrologer, but would haï؟½ sworn heï؟½d foreseen it at the last versary of Venus, when Vulcan caught her with Mars in the house of stinking Capricorn. But since ï؟½tis Jack of the Straw that hangs, the forgetful stars had it not on their tablets.
آ آ آ آ Prince. Another life! Were there any left to die? How did the poor fool come by it?
آ آ آ آ Gow. Simpliciter thus. She that damned him to death knew not that she did it, or would have died ere she had done it. For she loved him. He that hangs him does so in obedience to the Duke, and asks no more than ï؟½Where is the rope?ï؟½ The Duke, very exactly he hath told us, works Godï؟½s will, in which holy employ heï؟½s not to be questioned. We have then left upon this finger, only Jack whose soul now plucks the left sleeve of Destiny in Hell to overtake why she clapped him up like a fly on a sunny wall. Whuff! Soh!
آ آ آ آ Prince. Your cloak Ferdinand. Iï؟½ll sleep now.
آ آ آ آ Ferdinand. Sleep, then . . . He, too, loved his life?
آ آ آ آ Gow. He was born of woman . . . but at the end threw life from him like your Prince, for a little sleep . . ï؟½Have I any look of a King?ï؟½ said he, clanking his chain, ï؟½to be so baited on all sides by Fortune, that I must eï؟½en die now to live with myself one day longer.ï؟½ I left him railing at Fortune and womanï؟½s love.
آ آ آ آ Ferdinand. Ah, womanï؟½s love!
آ آ آ آ (Aside) Who knows not Fortune, glutted on easy thrones,
آ آ آ آ Stealing from feasts as rare to coneycatch
آ آ آ آ Privily in the hedgerows for a clown
آ آ آ آ With that same cruel-lustful hand and eye,
آ آ آ آ Those nails and wedges, that one hammer and lead,
آ آ آ آ And the very gerb of long-stored lightnings loosed
آ آ آ آ Yesterday ï؟½gainst some King.
meaning:
Rudyard Kipling's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "From Lydenï؟½s ï؟½Ireniusï؟½ - Act III. Sc. II."...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
